note: PLAN-3 R3. The inland sea's traffic is dense, short-haul, and mostly outside the northern toll series — hence the high share of reconstructed/asserted systems. Includes the Black Sea slave trade under the charter's sober-framing rule (R3 decision 4).
anchors: F. Braudel, La Méditerranée (the structural picture)
Levant Company records; the French échelles du Levant series (Marseille chamber of commerce)
D. Sella, R. Rapp (Venice); F. W. Carter (Ragusa)

ports.name: La Calle (the French concession; Bastion de France to 1677)

systems.name: The Levant trade
systems.basis: French échelles series (the dominant carrier by the 18th c) + Levant Company and Dutch directie records; silk, mohair, cotton, and drugs against cloth and silver.

systems.name: The Italian grain redistribution
systems.basis: Braudel and the annona studies: Sicilian and Puglian wheat feeding the Italian cities, with Egyptian grain north in dearth years.

systems.name: Venice and the eastern sea
systems.basis: Sella, Rapp, Tenenti: the Serenissima's long decline from spice emporium to regional carrier — visible here as a falling curve, not a disappearance.

systems.name: The Ragusan carrying trade
systems.basis: Carter; the neutral argosies at their 16th-c peak, broken by the 1667 earthquake, reviving under 18th-c neutrality.

systems.name: Marseille and the western basin
systems.basis: Marseille chamber of commerce series: the coasting and Spanish/Italian exchange beneath the Levant headline.

systems.name: The provisioning of Istanbul
systems.basis: The capital's grain lifeline — Egyptian wheat and Black Sea grain into the largest city of Europe and the Near East. The system the European commercial record cannot rank (the R1 declared boundary), quantified here from Ottoman provisioning scholarship.

systems.name: Greek and Ottoman coasting
systems.basis: ASSERTED — the record's blind spot: the caïques and polaccas of Greek, Armenian, and Turkish shippers who carried the eastern basin's daily exchange under flags no western customs house logged. Central guesses scale with the known ports' consumption; bounds ±60%. By the 1780s the Greek merchant marine is large enough to enter western records — the curve rises accordingly.

systems.name: The Habsburg silver route (Barcelona–Genoa)
systems.basis: Braudel's 'Spanish road' at sea: silver, troops, and credit between Iberia and Habsburg Italy, ending with the dynasty's Mediterranean system.

systems.name: The Black Sea slave trade (Kaffa)
systems.basis: The Crimean Khanate's raiding economy shipped captives from Kaffa to Ottoman markets for two centuries — a coerced flow the charter forbids leaving silently zero (R3 decision 4). Volume reconstructions from Ottoman customs studies; declines with the raids and ends with Russian annexation (1783).

framing.label: coerced human movement
framing.description: Captive people carried across the Black Sea to Ottoman slave markets — the northern counterpart of the trans-Saharan routes, and for the 16th–17th centuries among the largest coerced flows anywhere.
framing.rule: No value framing, no profit framing, never a reward. Sim-side representation waits for S2's generalization of the Middle-Passage invariant; until then these flows exist as historical record only.

systems.name: The French Barbary concessions
systems.basis: European company bookkeeping only, and deliberately narrow: the Bastion de France (built 1561), its successor establishment at La Calle (1677), and the Compagnie royale d'Afrique (Constantinois grain and coral, chartered 1741, DISSOLVED 1793). These are the one cleanly counted Barbary flow because a European company kept the books. They must NOT be read as the volume of Maghrib commerce - the bulk of that trade is reconstructed in barbary-regency-exports or gestured in the register. Guarded against double-counting with marseille-trade: these are the concession's own shipping, not Marseille's general Levant business.
systems.notes: Ends 1793 with the Compagnie royale's dissolution, NOT 1798 (a date that circulates but does not match the company's own record). The concession sites changed hands and were sacked more than once; the decade dips (1640s-50s) are those interruptions, not a data gap.

systems.name: The Genoese coral concession at Tabarka
systems.basis: The Lomellini family concession at Tabarka (1540-1742), Genoa-facing, worked for coral. Company bookkeeping again, hence counted; the volumes are small and the lane is a single short run. Split out from barbary-concessions rather than folded into it because Tabarka was annexed by Tunis in 1742 and its population deported - a system running to 1793 would have kept a Genoese lane alive on a place that had ceased to exist.

systems.name: Regency exports: grain, oil, wool, hides, wax, caps
systems.basis: The Regencies of Algiers, Tunis, and Tripoli exported grain, oil, wool, hides, wax, and the Tunisian chechia cap to European and Levantine termini throughout the era; Tripoli was also the maritime terminus of a Saharan caravan trade. No published decade series of SAILINGS exists, so the volumes here are reconstructed from commodity descriptions and the size of the receiving markets, with wide bounds. A circulating figure of ~110,000 dozen chechias a year at Tunis is single-source and was NOT used to set these numbers; it needs Valensi or Boubaker behind it before it can carry any weight.
systems.notes: The name describes the system's origin, not its whole span: Algiers ceased to be a Regency at the French conquest of 1830, and the post-1830 Algiers-Marseille lanes are French-Algerian traffic carrying the same goods. The lane is real across the whole era; only the flag over it changes, which belongs to the port's eraPowers rather than here. Tunis and Tripoli remained Regencies past 1850.
